content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Admissions and intake-equity regulation (keep structure, fix the gate)'. Hungary's early-selection system at ages 10–12 increases achievement inequality and socio-economic stratification without raising average performance, and similar learning gains to Portugal's are achievable through non-structural reforms (curriculum clarity, assessment transparency, retention reduction, targeted support) within a comprehensive framework.
opportunities: Reduced SES-skew in admitted cohorts at the 6/8-year gate, addressing the documented near-zero intake share of multiply-disadvantaged pupils.
Preserves the approximately 10% competency-gain advantage associated with 6/8-year tracks for admitted students, since the track structure itself is untouched.
strengths: Portugal maintained a comprehensive (non-selective) school structure through compulsory basic education and improved PISA results by approximately 31 points (0.3 standard deviations) between 2000 and 2015–2018 without introducing early tracking.

Portugal's documented gains in learning outcomes resulted from curriculum standards clarification, increased school autonomy, regular student assessment, and vocational pathway development—not from changes to selection age.
threats: Implementation by ministerial decree rather than cardinal law leaves the mechanism exposed to reversal by a subsequent government within a single electoral cycle; the scenario does not propose a specific entrenchment design (e.g., cardinal-law status, cross-party pact, or sunset-clause safeguard) to mitigate this.
Fidesz's demonstrated framing of any selection-related change as ideologically totalitarian creates reversal risk even for a narrower admissions-only intervention, not only for structural abolition.
weaknesses: Whether targeted support (TEIP-type) can close achievement gaps rather than merely reducing dropout rates remains uncertain because no longitudinal study has tracked student competency trajectories beyond retention reduction.

The fiscal and pedagogical cost of phase-out or structural reform of Hungary's 8-year grammar school track (replacing it with comprehensive entry and specialist bands at 14+) has not been modeled in published Hungarian research.
